reference: Hi. This is most probably a condition called gynecomastia, and the best course of action is to have this operated on, removed, and subjected to histopathological examination. Although this is usually a bilateral phenomenon, it is not rare to see it like this. Cancer has to be ruled out by performing histopathology on the removed specimen, as X-rays do not play a significant role in the diagnosis. Radiography in the form of a computed tomography scan of the lesion, chest, and axillae is helpful.
text: Hi, this is most probably a condition called gynecomastia, and the best course of action is to have this operated on, removed, and subjected to histopathological examination. Although this is usually a bilateral phenomenon, it is not rare to see it like this. Cancer has to be ruled out by performing histopathology on the removed specimen, as X-rays do not play a significant role in the diagnosis. Radiography in the form of a computed tomography scan of the lesion, chest, and axillary is helpful.
